coredata.dc:title: Concentration-dependent and pH response carbon dots and application in latent fingerprints recognition
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Xiangjun
Jia, Huiying
Zhang, Hongmei
Xu, Jiyao
Zhao, Xihui
coredata.dc:description: Herein, red fluorescence carbon dots (R-CDs) were synthesized via solvothermal method using citric acid and urea as raw materials in DMF solvent. What is more interesting is that the synthesized CDs displayed concentration-dependent photoluminescence behavior, which can be ascribe to the fact that the intermolecular forces pulled the CDs particles closer, and the functional groups on the CDs surface combined together, thereby narrowing the energy gap, which ultimately led to the fluorescence emission peak moving to longer wavelength with the increase of concentration accompanied by a significant reduction in the fluorescent intensity. In addition, the diluted CDs displayed pH response. With the continuous increase of pH, the color of the solution changes from green to pink, and the fluorescence intensity increases significantly, which has a linear relationship in the range of pH 8–14. These characteristics are conducive to the visual detection of pH value. Moreover, in order to overcome aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), a solid phosphor with an emission peak at 600 nm was obtained by incorporating chitosan matrix, which showed excellent performance in latent fingerprints (LFPs) detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Concentration-dependent
PH response
Fingerprint detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of concentration-dependent CDs and Fluorometric determination of pH
Optical properties of R-CDs and S-R-CDs
Concentration-dependent behavior of the synthesized CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province ( ZR202110280005 ), and State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and EcoTextiles ( Qingdao University ) ( ZKT41 and ZFZ201815 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images, (B) Size distribution, (C) XRD, (D) FT-IR, (E) Raman spectrum of R-CDs. (F) XPS survey spectrum and (G-I) High-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively.

(A) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence spectra of R-CDs. Inset: photographs under sunlight and 365 nm UV light. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of R-CDs under different excitation wavelengths. (C) CIE chromaticity coordinates of R-CDs. (D) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of S-R-CDs. Inset: photographs of S-R-CDs under sunlight and 365 nm UV light. (E) Fluorescence emission spectra of S-R-CDs under different excitation wavelengths. (F) Fluorescence decay curve of the S-R-CDs.

(A) The optimal emission spectra of A1-A6. (B-G) FL emission spectra of CDs diluted in DMF with different concentrations (A1 to A6) under different excitation wavelengths. (H) Photographs of CDs at different concentrations from A1 to A6 under 365 nm UV light (I) Schematic diagram of the dominant emission color of the CDs depending on the interparticle distance.

(A-C) FL emission spectra of CDs solutions under pH= 2, pH= 9 and pH= 13 conditions, respectively. (D) CIE chromaticity coordinates of CDs solutions under acid to alkaline conditions. Inset: photograph of CDs solution under 365 nm UV lamp. (E) FL emission spectra of CDs at different pH values. (F) Fitted plot of PL intensity and pH value, the inset displays the fitting equation.

(A) Photographs of S-R-CDs phosphor-stained LFPs under sunlight and 365 nm UV light, magnified images of A: (b1) core, (b2) bifurcate, (b3) type lines, (b4) termination, (b5) enclosure, (b6) crossover. (B) Fluorescent images of LFPs on different substrate surfaces under sunlight and 365 nm UV light.
The diagram of synthesis and applications of R-CDs.

head.ce:title: Concentration-dependent and pH response carbon dots and application in latent fingerprints recognition

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review &editing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, red fluorescence carbon dots (R-CDs) were synthesized via solvothermal method using citric acid and urea as raw materials in DMF solvent. What is more interesting is that the synthesized CDs displayed concentration-dependent photoluminescence behavior, which can be ascribe to the fact that the intermolecular forces pulled the CDs particles closer, and the functional groups on the CDs surface combined together, thereby narrowing the energy gap, which ultimately led to the fluorescence emission peak moving to longer wavelength with the increase of concentration accompanied by a significant reduction in the fluorescent intensity. In addition, the diluted CDs displayed pH response. With the continuous increase of pH, the color of the solution changes from green to pink, and the fluorescence intensity increases significantly, which has a linear relationship in the range of pH 8–14. These characteristics are conducive to the visual detection of pH value. Moreover, in order to overcome aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), a solid phosphor with an emission peak at 600 nm was obtained by incorporating chitosan matrix, which showed excellent performance in latent fingerprints (LFPs) detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an emerging luminescent nanomaterial, CDs have broad application prospects in the fields of optical devices, biosensing, anti-counterfeiting, and fingerprint recognition. Compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots, CDs have the characteristics of good water solubility, low cost of raw materials, and good biological compatibility [1,2]. However, most CDs only exhibit short wave emission, such as blue and green, which greatly limits their applications in polychromatic patterns, light emitting diodes, and bioimaging, etc.[3]. With the continuous and in-depth research of CDs, multicolor fluorescent CDs have been successfully synthesized by tuning particle size, carbon source, changing the reaction conditions such as the ratio of precursor reactants, the amount of solvent, or by doping heteroatoms to change the chemical structure and their luminescence mechanism has also been discussed [4,5]. For instance, Lu et al. used ethanol and sulfuric acid to synthesize CDs, and obtained three colors CDs (blue, green and yellow) through varying their size by column chromatography purification [6]. Shang et al. prepared different emission CDs covering the full spectrum by adjusting the ratio of asparagine and p-phenylenediamine. The unique emission characteristics were attributed to the difference in the nitrogen content of the CDs [7]. Regardless of these views, a more interesting phenomenon is that the luminescence of CDs can be adjusted by changing the concentration, thereby changing the intermolecular interaction and even the surface electron distribution. Chen et al. synthesized CDs via a one pot hydrothermal synthesis and re-dispersed in dimethylformamide solution after purification. The emission wavelength of the carbon dots with different concentrations can be tuned from 585 to 514 nm [8]. Shi et al. presented a green and facile strategy for fabrication of concentration-dependent multicolor fluorescent CDs by using coffee, salvia, and urea as the starting material. The obtained CDs emit three different colors of fluorescence (i.e. orange, yellow, and blue) with their concentration reduction[9]. Although many efforts have been made to modulate the photoluminescence (PL) of CDs and obtain polychromatic emission, the development of the facile synthesis of concentration tunable multicolor emission CDs is still highly desired.

In addition, in the application of pH sensing, CDs have attracted wide attention because of their simple preparation, fast detection speed and high sensitivity [10,11]. In some literatures, the change of pH value is accompanied not only by the change of intensity, but also by the change of color. These features play an important role in promoting the development of visual inspection [12]. For example, Wang et al. synthesized a variety of CDs for detecting pH and monitoring enzyme reactions [13]. Hu et al. prepared CDs with pH responsive behavior through citric acid reaction and used them for the detection of biogenic amines, which has great application prospects in the detection of some proteins [14]. Therefore, it is of great significance to explore a simple and intuitive pH detection method by using the response property of CDs to pH.

Besides, although CDs-based LFPs detection has been widely reported, these results are mainly based on CDs solutions [15]. However, For LFPs detection, aqueous solution CDs have poor stability and are not easy to observe on some substrates [16]. In contrast, solid-state powders markers can more clearly display fingerprints by adhering to human skin oils remaining on the surface of objects. However, the ACQ properties of pure solid-state CDs limit their application in LFPs detection [17]. In order to overcome this problem, some organic and inorganic materials, such as nano-silica, BaSO4, starch, have been used to disperse CDs to overcome aggregation, which not only keeps the PL property of CDs in the desired solid state, but also endows CDs composites uniform particles and good fluidity, which is conducive to LFPs detection [18,19]. For example, Zhai et al. synthesized carbon dots@montmorillonite solid-state composites and further applied for LFPs markers [20]. David et al. confined CDs in a matrix-framed porous material to produce a blue-emitting composite to detect LFPs [21]. However, only a few studies have been reported on red emission of CDs, it is desirable and highly worthy to obtain solid-state CDs with red luminescence.

In this work, we synthesized CDs with red fluorescence (R-CDs) via citric acid and urea as substrates and DMF as solvent. Interestingly, the obtained R-CDs solution presented concentration-dependent emission property and exhibited almost full spectrum fluorescence as continuously diluted by DMF. That is the PL emission from red to blue can be tuned by interparticle distance, which can be realized simply by diluting aqueous solution of the R-CDs. Additionally, R-CDs solution diluted with water display pH response behavior, showing not only changes in fluorescence intensity but also changes in fluorescence color at different pH values. Furthermore, solid-state red fluorescence CDs-based composites named S-R-CDs were obtained by mixing R-CDs with Chitosan. R-CDs were uniformly dispersed in the resulting S-R-CDs solid-state composites, which not only efficiently suppress the ACQ of CDs, but also have good fluidity, strong fluorescence, and can clearly display fingerprints under various substrates. The diagram of synthesis and applications is shown in Scheme 1.

Citric acid, urea, chitosan, sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shenyang, China). N. N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) was purchased from Shanghai Teton Technology Co., Ltd. and can be used without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the experiment.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images testing were performed using a JEM-1200 EX (Japan, JEOL) transmission electron microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was recorded with X′ Pert PRO MPD, measuring with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) in the 2θ range of 10–90°. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was presented on a spectrometer (Nexus 670) with scanning range of 500–4000 cm−1 and a resolution of 4 cm−1. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was tested on ESCALAB 250 X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy. Fluorescence (FL) was carried out on a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA, Kyoto, Japan). The UV–vis absorbance was measured in TU-1901 (Beijing, China), and the fluorescence attenuation curves was obtained with Edinburgh FLS980. Particle sizes and zeta potential were analyzed with Zeta sizer nano dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Malvern, UK).

R-CDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. Specifically, 1.2 g citric acid and 2.4 g urea were dissolved in 10 mL DMF and completely dissolved after 15 min of ultrasonic treatment. Afterwards, transferred the obtained mixed solution to a 20 mL Teflon autoclave and kept it at 160 °C for 4 h. After the reaction, mixed the obtained dark brown solution with 20 mL NaOH solution (50 mg/mL), then added 20 mL ethanol and stirred for 5 min. The mixture was then centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 20 min, the solids were collected, dissolved in water to remove residual alkali, and centrifuged again. Finally, purple black solid named R-CDs was obtained by freeze-drying.

Solid-state red fluorescence CDs-based composites named S-R-CDs were prepared as follows: 0.05 g solid R-CDs and 5 g chitosan were mixed and dissolved in 100 mL water, and then ultrasonic treatment was performed until the mixture was uniform. Finally, the mixture was dried in an oven at 70 °C for 6 h to obtain S-R-CDs phosphor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of concentration-dependent CDs and Fluorometric determination of pH
ce:section.ce:para: The dark brown solution obtained after carbonization was filtered through a microporous filter (0.22 µm), and the obtained solution was denoted as A1. Diluted A1 with DMF, and the dilution times are 2, 4, 10, 100, and 400 times in turn, which are named as A2, A3, A4, A5, and A6 respectively. Then, PL spectra of CDs with different concentrations were recorded by fluorescence spectroscopy.

The obtained CDs were diluted 200 folds with water and then adjusted with NaOH or HCl to obtain a series of CDs solutions with different pH values. And then the fluorescence spectra of the obtained series of pH solutions were measured in the wavelength range of 490–570 nm. The excitation wavelength was 470 nm, and the excitation and emission slits were set at 3 nm.

Fingerprint samples were taken from the right index finger of the male donor. First, washed your hands with soap before touching various substrates, and then gently wiped your hands on your forehead. Secondly, sprayed the powder on the base and lightly brushed away the excess powder with a small brush. Finally, the red fingerprint mark was clearly visible under sunlight and 365 nm light. The substrates used include glass slides, plastic plates, crucible covers, weighing paper and stainless steel.

The size and morphology of R-CDs were obtained by TEM. The TEM image in Fig. 1A displayed that the prepared R-CDs were spherical and monodispersed nanoparticles. And the inset HRTEM image of R-CDs presented well-resolved lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which contributes to the (100) facet of graphene [22]. From the size distribution histogram in Fig. 1B, it can be seen that the size of R-CDs ranges from 1.5 to 4.5 nm with an average size about 2.9 nm. The XRD pattern (Fig. 1C) revealed a narrow and strong peak around 26.7° and corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite [23,24]. The composition of R-CDs was characterized by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 1D, the surfaces of the R-CDs samples are rich in hydrophilic groups such as O-H (3428 cm−1) and amino/amide N-H bonds (3344 cm−1). The C-H vibrational absorption bands at 2931 and 2848 cm−1 may be related to the amide carbonyl. Characteristic peaks located at 1594 and 1668 cm−1 are due to CC/CN stretching vibrations and the presence of COO-, which means that dehydration and condensation reactions occurred during carbonization[3]. In addition, it can be seen from the FT-IR spectrum that the R-CDs contain C-N (1436 cm−1), C-H (882 cm−1) and C-O (1055 cm−1) groups [25,26]. The Raman spectrum of R-CDs in Fig. 1E exhibited two typical scattering peaks at 1569 and 1344 cm−1, corresponding to graphitic sp2 carbon structures (G band) and disordered sp3 carbon structures (D band). The ID/IG ratio is 1.03, demonstrating the high graphitization degree of R-CDs [27]. The results were agreed with the TEM and XRD results.

Meanwhile, element composition and valence state of R-CDs was further characterized by XPS. As illustrated in Fig. 1 F, the XPS full spectrum has four typical peaks at 284.2 eV, 399.2 eV, 531.2 eV and 1070.2 eV, which correspond to C1s, N1s, O1s and Na 1 s, respectively, demonstrating the R-CDs were mainly composed of C, O N and Na elements and the elemental composition ratio was calculated to be C 55.76%, O 35.40%, N 0.66% and Na 8.17%[28,29]. The appearance of NaKLL signal in XPS indicates that the sample may contain sodium carboxylate, which is consistent with the results in FT-IR[3]. In Fig. 1G-I, the high-resolution C1s spectra of R-CDs can be decomposed into three peaks at 284.84 eV, 285.88 eV and 289.37 eV, which correspond to sp2 C (CC), sp3 C (C-C/C-N), and CO groups, respectively[30]. The O1s had three peaks with binding energies of 531.26 eV, 532.84 eV and 535.6 eV, respectively, which proving the existence of CO, C-O groups and adsorbed water[31,32]. The N1s can be fitted into two peaks at 399.73 eV and 400.32 eV, respectively, which correspond to pyrrolic N and graphitic-like/amino N groups[33]. From the analysis results of FT-IR and XPS, it is known that nitrogen was successfully incorporated into the R-CDs and the surface of R-CDs is rich in function groups including hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino and so on.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of R-CDs and S-R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV-Vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy for exploring the optical properties of R-CDs. It can be seen that the R-CDs are brown-red dark solutions under sunlight and pink under 365 nm UV light irradiation ( Fig. 2 A). The UV-Vis absorption of R-CDs has a strong absorption peak at 330 nm, which correspond to the n-π * transition of the CO bond [34]. Another broad absorption peak at 490 nm is attributed to the defect state transitions induced by surface functional groups. These groups can be amine groups, hydroxyl groups, or other oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs [23,35]. It is worth nothing that, the PL excitation spectrum (blue line in Fig. 2A) of the R-CDs overlapped with the absorption spectrum in the long wavelength range, indicating that the defect states from the surface functional groups is favorable for the red emission of the samples [36]. And the optimal PL emission peak of R-CDs is around 620 nm (red line in Fig. 2A). Furthermore, chitosan was added to the R-CDs solution, and then dried to obtain S-R-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2D, S-R-CDs are rose red powder under sunlight and pink fluorescence at 365 nm. At the same time, the optimal excitation peak is located at about 550 nm, and the maximum emission peak is around 600 nm, which has a smaller Stokes shift compared with R-CDs. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 2B, when the excitation wavelength was increased from 380 nm to 570 nm, the PL emission peaks of R-CDs and S-R-CD were slightly shifted at 600 and 620 nm, respectively, both showing weak excitation dependence, which may be related to the uniformity of size and surface state of R-CDs[7]. The emission color of R-CD was further investigated by chromaticity diagram (CIE) (Fig. 2C), the emission wavelength shifted slightly to red as the excitation wavelength increased. And the CIE coordinates of optimal emission is (0.62, 0.38). Furthermore, the PL decay curve is obtained by fitting the double exponential function, as shown in Fig. 2F, and the fluorescence lifetime of S-R-CDs is 3.4 ns[37]. In addition, with Rhodamine B (56%) as the standard and the excitation wavelength of 540 nm, the quantum yield of R-CDs was calculated to be 5.8% [38]. Furthermore, the stability of salt solution concentration (0.5–2.0 M NaCl) and 365 UV lamp irradiation (0–2 h) was investigated. As shown in Fig. S1A-B, the fluorescence intensity did not change significantly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concentration-dependent behavior of the synthesized CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Through the data analysis of PL spectra, the synthesized CDs may have different emission centers, so CDs solutions with different concentrations were prepared [39]. As shown in Fig. S2, with the increase of the concentration, the optimal excitation wavelength of the sample gradually increased from 420 nm to 470 nm, and the half-width of the excitation peak became larger and larger. The corresponding optimal emission peak position has also been red-shifted. From the initial 500–610 nm, covering the emission region from blue to red, and at the high concentration (A4-A6), a new red range emission bands at around 610 nm appear and enhance gradually ( Fig. 3A). Moreover, as can be seen in Fig. 3B-G, When the concentration is 0.75 mg/mL, under short-wave excitation, it shows a wider half-peak width, the best excitation and emission wavelength are 420 nm and 500 nm, respectively, and exhibit a strong cyan fluorescence under ultraviolet light with significant excitation dependence. Compared with A1, A2 with a concentration of 2 mg/mL has a slight red shift, and the optimal excitation peak position is shifted by 30 nm. The concentration of A3 is 15 mg/mL, with the increase of excitation wavelength, there is obvious excitation dependence, and the optimal emission peak position is at 510 nm. For A4-A6, there is no obvious excitation dependence, with the concentration from 37 to 98–176 mg/mL, the peak intensity at 500 nm gradually weakens, conversely, the peak intensity at 610 nm gradually increases, and the optimal excitation wavelengths are all at 470 nm. At the same time, from the CIE coordinate diagram in Fig. S3, it can be seen that the color has a large span with the change of concentration. And the fluorescence color of A1-A6 ranges from blue to pale yellow to orange-red under 365 nm UV lamp, covering almost the full spectrum (Fig. 3H). It is concluded that the obtained CDs not only possess obvious excitation-dependent but also concentration-dependent emission properties, indicating that they have great application value in sensing, labeling, and so on [40]. The concentration-dependent properties of CDs may be controlled by the interaction among the particles (Fig. 3I). The surface states of CDs are regulated by carbonyl, hydroxy and amino and account for the fluorescence emission process. At lower concentration, the interparticle distance increases, leading to the decrease of the interaction among the particles, as well as a blue-shift and increase of PL intensity [41]. While, at higher concentration, the intermolecular forces pull the CDs particles closer together, and the functional groups on the surface of CDs bind together, thus narrowing the energy gap, which ultimately results in a red-shift and decrease of PL intensity [36,42]. Additionally, based on the different colors of CDs at different concentrations, it shows great application potential in multi-color fluorescent ink. As shown in Fig. S4, by pressing the numbers with different concentrations of CDs on the filter paper, the multi-color numbers from dark blue to green and then to orange can be seen under 365 nm UV light.

The prepared CDs solution showed obvious pH response [43]. As shown in Fig. 4A-C, first, under acid (pH=2) conditions, it is a single peak emission. With the increase of excitation wavelength, the emission peak has an obvious red shift. The best emission peak is at 540 nm, and it is green under 365 nm UV lamp. Secondly, under weak alkaline (pH=9) conditions, it has a large a large full width at half maximum of 470–620 nm. Therefore, at 365 nm UV lamp, the color is a composite color, close to white. When the excitation wavelength is greater than 470 nm, it is a single peak emission with obvious excitation dependence. Whereas, under strong alkaline (pH=13) conditions, it is an obvious bimodal emission at around 470 nm and 600 nm. The emission peak at low wavelength has an obvious excitation dependence, while the position of the emission peak at 600 nm remains unchanged. With the increase of the excitation wavelength, the fluorescence intensity gradually increases. Thus, under the 365 nm UV lamp, it is pink, that is a combination of blue and red. The photos of CDs with different colors at 365 nm UV lamp under acid to alkaline conditions were shown in the inset of Fig. 4D. According to the fluorescence data, the corresponding CIE coordinates of CDs under the optimal emission conditions were obtained, which were (0.27, 0.44), (0.28, 0.34) and (0.40, 0.33) under acid, weak alkaline and strong alkaline conditions, respectively (Fig. 4D).

Fig. 4E presents the fluorescence spectra of CDs solutions with different pH values under 470 nm excitation. It can be clearly seen that when the pH value is between acid and weak alkaline, the optimal emission peak position is around 520 nm, and the fluorescence intensity increases first and then decreases slightly. When the pH value is strong alkaline, a new peak appears at 600 nm and the fluorescence is enhanced with the continuous increase of peak intensity. In the whole pH range, the luminescence intensity under alkaline conditions is significantly higher than that under acid conditions, which may be due to H aggregation under acid conditions, leading to fluorescence quenching [14,44]. Moreover, it can be seen from Fig. S5 that the particle size of CDs increases significantly with the decrease of pH value. However, under alkaline conditions, the fluorescence intensity of the optimal emission peak continuously increases. This is because the shift of Fermi level and the presence of surface charge leads to the reduction of electronic transitions at long wavelengths, which in turn leads to the significant enhancement of red emission. Additionally, when the pH value of the solution is from 2 to 13, the zeta potential changes from − 12.8 mV to − 45.3 mV, which is related to the protonation and deprotonation of the surface of CDs (Fig. S6). Through the analysis of zeta potential and DLS results, CDs under acidic conditions prefer aggregation, which further leads to different colors at different pH [11,14]. When studying the linear relationship between different pH values and fluorescence intensity, it can be seen from Fig. 4F that there is a good linear relationship in the range of pH 1–4, and the equation is
Y= 72386.2667 x+ 168041.6467
(R2 =0.9870). When the pH value range is 8–14, the exponential growth function in the nonlinear fitting is used:
Y=
Y
0
+a*
e
R
0
X
, where Y0, a and R0 are constants, x is the pH value, and Y is the corresponding PL intensity [10,24]. At this time, the equation is
Y=- 48702.8164 + a 6350.8109 *
e
0.4233 X
(R2 = 0.9810). Based on the great difference in fluorescence intensity and color of CDs at different pH values, it can be applied to visual detection of pH values, which has important practical significance in real life.

In daily life, there is no obvious optical contrast between the oil or sweat on the fingertip and the surface of the contact object, so the fingerprint can hardly be seen [45]. However, fingerprints are unique and often used as important evidence to arrest some criminals. It is urgent for us to find a simple developing method to identify fingerprints. However, due to ACQ the prepared solid-state CDs cannot be used as a high-quality fingerprint powder[18]. 0.5 mg/mL R-CDs solution has the strongest fluorescence as shown in Fig. S7, and then it is mixed with chitosan to obtain solid red fluorescent powder (S-R-CDs) and further used to identify LFPs. It must be noted that compared with other visual phosphors, chitosan as a matrix has less harm to human body. As shown in Fig. 5A, under the irradiation of sunlight and 365 nm ultraviolet light, the ridges of the fingerprint image of S-R-CDs powder adhered to the glass plate are clear, and the main feature points such as rings and lines can be clearly seen, and the pink color is easy to see. At the same time, the enlarged regional image can also clearly see the core, bifurcation, island, termination, surrounding and intersection of the fingerprint[45,46]. These secondary feature points provide an important basis for fingerprint analysis [47]. In addition, we used some common substrates, such as ceramics, plastic plates, weighing paper and stainless steel, to obtain the effect of phosphors obtained in different environments on LFPs detection [17]. The results are shown in Fig. 5B. Under sunlight and ultraviolet light, some local ridge feature points of the fingerprint can be clearly seen in the LFPs image of the same donor, and the images of various substrates are clear. In addition, we also measured the stability of S-R-CDs powder under ultraviolet light and different temperatures. As shown in Fig. S8A-B., the fluorescence intensity did not change significantly under long-term ultraviolet light and high temperatures. The above results indicated the great potential of the synthesized S-R-CDs in practical applications of LFPs detection.

In summary, R-CDs with red fluorescence were synthesized via solvothermal method using citric acid and urea in DMF solvent. Interestingly, the prepared CDs displayed concentration dependent emission behavior, exhibiting almost the full spectrum of colors from blue to orange-red from low concentration to high concentration, which may be related to the intermolecular forces pulling the CDs particles closer, and the functional groups on the surface of CDs bind together, thus narrowing the energy gap, which ultimately results in a red-shift and decrease of PL intensity. Additionally, the diluted CDs displayed pH response, accompanied by changes in color and fluorescence intensity. In acid condition, the H aggregation induce the fluorescence quenching, meanwhile, in alkaline condition, the Fermi level decreased due to the electron-rich hydroxide radicals. As a result, the fluorescence intensity of CDs in strong alkaline solution is significantly higher than that in the strong acid solution. Based on the above properties of CDs, it can be applied to the visual detection of pH. Furthermore, the obtained S-R-CDs powder by mixing R-CDs with chitosan has good dispersibility, easy preparation, non-toxicity and can also be applied to LFPs detection, demonstrating their great practical value.

Xiangjun Guo: Data curation, Writing, original draft preparation. Huiying Jia: Formal analysis. Hongmei Zhang: Formal analysis. Jiyao Xu: Formal analysis. Xihui Zhao: Investigation. Xihui Zhao: Methodology. Xihui Zhao: Project administration. Xihui Zhao: Resources. Xihui Zhao: Supervision. Xihui Zhao: Writing – review & editing..

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR202110280005), and State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and EcoTextiles (Qingdao University) (ZKT41 and ZFZ201815).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.130426.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
